coredata.dc:title: Nitrogen-Doped Graphdiyne Quantum-dots as an Optical-Electrochemical sensor for sensitive detection of dopamine
coredata.dc:creator: Bai, Qiang
Luo, Hongyang
Yi, Xuetao
Shi, Shugao
Wang, Lina
Liu, Manhong
Du, Fanglin
Yang, Zhugen
Sui, Ning
coredata.dc:description: Graphdiyne quantum dots (GDQDs) have attracted increasing attentions due to its unique electronic, optical, and electrochemical properties. However, the low conductivity and quantum yield of GDQDs limit their application. Here, nitrogen-doped graphdiyne dots (N-GDQDs) are firstly synthesized by a simple, friendly and one-step hydrothermal method. The N-GDQDs show a maximum emission at 410 nm under the excitation wavelength of 319 nm. The doping N modifies the surface defect of N-GDQDs and further greatly improves their quantum yield (from 14.6% to 48.6%). In addition, the doping N induces a strong electron transport ability and good conductivity of N-GDQDs. Subsequently, the prepared N-GDQDs are used for constructing an optical-electrochemical nanosensor for sensitive and selective detection of dopamine (DA). DA can quench the fluorescence of N-GDQDs by forming a ground-state non-fluorescent complex between phenoxy anions (in PBS solution) in DA and pyridinic N sites of N-GDQDs, which leads to a highly sensitive and selective detection of DA with a limit of detection (LOD) of 0.14 μM and a linear range of 0.32–500 μM. In the electrochemical detection, DA can be oxidized to DA-quinone under the electric field through N-GDQDs/GCE, which shows a big affinity to N-GDQDs. The LOD for DA is 0.02 μM with a linear range of 0.05–240 μM. Finally, the spiked application for DA detection in human serum samples is investigated, the results show that the method has high accuracy. Our work provides a new carbon quantum dots based sensing platform, which shows great potential in practical application.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphdiyne dots
Nitrogen-doped
Fluorescence
Electrochemical
Dopamine

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Application test of DA in spiked sample testing
The mechanism of the N-GDQDs sensor to DA in optical and electrochemical mode
Spiked application of N-GDQDs for analysis of DA in human serum samples

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: characterization of N-GDQDs, (a) Representative TEM image of N-GDQDs. Inset: size distribution of N-GDQDs. (b) High-resolution TEM image of N-GDQDs. (c) AFM image of N-GDQDs. (d) Raman spectroscopy of GDY, GDQDs and N-GDQDs. (e) C1s spectra of N-GDQDs. (f) N1s spectra of N-GDQDs.

optical properties of N-GDQDs, (a) UV − vis absorption spectra of N-GDQDs, inset: photographs of N-GDQDs solution (dispersed in water) under sunlight (left) and under 365 nm UV irradiation (right). (b) Excitation and emission spectra of N-GDQDs. (c) 3D fluorescence spectra of N-GDQDs. (d) Fluorescence spectra of N-GDQDs excited at different wavelengths. (e) Photo stability of N-GDQDs under irradiation for 7000 s. (f) Fluorescence intensity (at 410 nm) of N-GDQDs influenced by different concentrations of salt solutions. (n = 3).

electrochemical properties of N-GDQDs, (a) Cyclic voltammograms and (b) EIS of the different electrode in the solution of 3 mM K3[Fe(CN)6] containing 0.1 M KCl. (c) CV response of the different electrode in 0.1 M PBS (pH = 7.0) with the presence of 100 μM of DA; Scan rate: 100 mV s−1. (d) CV curves of N-GDQD/GCE electrode in 0.1 M PBS (pH = 7.0) containing 120 μM DA at different scan rates (20–160 mV s−1); inset: the plots of the anodic peak current vs. the square root of scan rate for DA.

analytical performance of the sensor, (a) DPV curves of N-GDQDs/GCE in 0.1 M PBS (pH = 7.0) for different concentrations of DA (0–320 µM). (b) The calibration plot of the concentration versus peak current (0.05–240 µM). (c) Fluorescence spectra of N-GDQDs for the detection of different concentrations of DA (from top to bottom: 0–600 µM); (d) Calibration curve of F0-F versus DA concentration and low concentration DA (0.32–500 µM). (n = 3).

(a) Electrochemical interference detection of N-GDQDs/GCE after adding different substances. (b, c) Long-term repeatability and stability study of N-GDQDs/GCE for 0.1 mM DA. The error bars represent the standard deviation of three measurements. (d) Fluorescence interference detection of N-GDQDs/GCE after adding different substances. The error bars represent the standard deviation of three measurements. (a: DA, b: KCl, c: NaCl, d: glycine, e: L-cystein, f: sodium nitrate, g: hydrogen peroxide, h: glucose, i: histamine, j: glutamic acid).

mechanism of the N-GDQDs sensor to DA, (a) Fluorescence response curves of N-GDQDs after addition of different biomolecules (150 μM). (b) The DA reaction process on N-GDQDs/GCE in Electrochemical detection.
Synthesis of N-GDQDs and electrochemical-fluorescent detection of DA.
Comparison of different methods for DA detection.
Electrochemical and fluorescent recoveries of DA in human serum samples (n = 3).

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: Human serum samples was obtained from Qingdao Central Hospital.

head.ce:title: Nitrogen-Doped Graphdiyne Quantum-dots as an Optical-Electrochemical sensor for sensitive detection of dopamine

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: Qiang Bai and Hongyang Luo contributed to this work equally.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Nitrogen-doped graphdiyne dots (N-GDQDs) are firstly synthesized by hydrothermal method. The prepared N-GDQDs are used for constructing a dual-mode optical-electrochemical nanosensor for sensitive and selective detection of dopamine (DA).

Graphdiyne quantum dots (GDQDs) have attracted increasing attentions due to its unique electronic, optical, and electrochemical properties. However, the low conductivity and quantum yield of GDQDs limit their application. Here, nitrogen-doped graphdiyne dots (N-GDQDs) are firstly synthesized by a simple, friendly and one-step hydrothermal method. The N-GDQDs show a maximum emission at 410 nm under the excitation wavelength of 319 nm. The doping N modifies the surface defect of N-GDQDs and further greatly improves their quantum yield (from 14.6% to 48.6%). In addition, the doping N induces a strong electron transport ability and good conductivity of N-GDQDs. Subsequently, the prepared N-GDQDs are used for constructing an optical-electrochemical nanosensor for sensitive and selective detection of dopamine (DA). DA can quench the fluorescence of N-GDQDs by forming a ground-state non-fluorescent complex between phenoxy anions (in PBS solution) in DA and pyridinic N sites of N-GDQDs, which leads to a highly sensitive and selective detection of DA with a limit of detection (LOD) of 0.14 μM and a linear range of 0.32–500 μM. In the electrochemical detection, DA can be oxidized to DA-quinone under the electric field through N-GDQDs/GCE, which shows a big affinity to N-GDQDs. The LOD for DA is 0.02 μM with a linear range of 0.05–240 μM. Finally, the spiked application for DA detection in human serum samples is investigated, the results show that the method has high accuracy. Our work provides a new carbon quantum dots based sensing platform, which shows great potential in practical application.

ce:section.ce:para: Dopamine (DA) is the most abundant catecholamine neurotransmitter in the brain. It exists in the central nervous system, renal system, cardiovascular system and hormonal system of mammals, and plays an exciting or inhibitory role in physiological activities [1–3]. Higher levels of DA can lead to increasing of heart rate, high blood pressure, heart failure and drug addiction [4]. Lower levels of DA may lead to stress. In addition, low DA levels are also associated with diseases such as Parkinson’s disease [5], schizophrenia [6], Alzheimer’s disease [7] and depression [8]. Obviously, in order to understand the biological function of DA and its related biological processes and mechanisms, it is necessary to carry out real-time determination of DA in vivo [3].

At present, many classical detection methods have been established for DA, such as fluorescence spectroscopy [9], ultraviolet colorimetric method [10], surface enhanced Raman spectroscopy [11], electrochemical method [12], high performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) [13] and so on.However, these single classical technologies have some shortcomings. For example, electrochemical detection is considered to be an effective detection method because of its simple operation, low cost, fast detection speed, high sensitivity and low detection limit, but its linear range is narrow [14–16]. The fluorescent method has a large detection range, and the process is simple and fast [17,18], but the sensitivity needs to be further improved [19,20]. Hence, the combination of fluorescent and electrochemical techniques can make up for the shortcomings of every single method, enabling sensitive and selective detection of DA for a wide range of application.

Graphdiyne (GDY) is a new two-dimensional carbon nanomaterial comprising sp2− and sp−hybridized carbon atoms with high π-conjugation. It has large triangular rings composed of 18 carbon atoms, which form uniformly distributed pores. Graphdiyne quantum dots (GDQDs) are a new type of zero-dimensional luminescent nanomaterials with great development prospects [21]. GDQDs exhibit stable luminescence, excellent biocompatibility and low toxicity, and have a great potential in biomedical sensors, medical imaging, electronics, semiconductors and other fields [21–23]. Nitrogen is a commonly used element doped in carbon-based materials because its atomic size and valence electrons can bind to carbon atoms [24]. Due to the quantum confinement effect of GDQDs and the presence of sp and sp2 sites, nitrogen-doped GDQDs may exhibit different optical properties and quantum yield by modulating the band gap, which was reported in N-doped graphene quantum dots (GQDs) [25]. The conductivity and electron transfer rate of GDQDs can be enhanced by doping N element. Because of the high π-conjugation conjugate structure of GDQDs, DA can be adsorbed by π-π stacking, and enhanced DA signal can be obtained due to its excellent electrical properties. Nitrogen can change the electronic structure of GDQDs, and the quantum yield can be enhanced from 14.6% [26] to 48.6%. High quantum yield can improve the detection sensitivity, thus improving the fluorescent property of GDQDs by doping N processes is of great significance.

To the best of our knowledge, this is the first report on N-doped GDQDs (N-GDQDs). Here, N-GDQDs are successfully prepared by hydrothermal method, and the doping N greatly enhances the quantum yield of N-GDQDs.In addition, through the study of the electrochemical properties of N-GDQDs, they have strong electron transport ability and good conductivity.On this basis, we design an optical-electrochemical sensor to detect DA, as shown in the Scheme. 1 . A non-fluorescent complex is formed between the N-GDQDs substance and DA to reduce fluorescence; under the action of electric field, DA is oxidized to DA-quinone by N-GDQDs/GCE to generate a current signal. The optical-electrochemical sensor has high detection sensitivity and a broad detection range.Finally, N-GDQDs are successfully applied to the quantitative determination of DA in human serum.Our work provides a new carbon quantum dot as an optical-electrochemical sensor for DA detection, which shows a good practical application prospect.

Urea (CH4N2O), dopamine (C8H11NO2), KCl, NaCl, glycine, L-cystein, sodium nitrate, hydrogen peroxide, glucose, histamine, glutamic acid, β-phenethylamine, tyramine and catechol were purchased from Sinopharmaceutical Group Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd·THF and TBAF were purchased from Aladin Ltd. All reagents and solvents were not further purified after purchase. Ultra-pure water was used throughout the experiment. Electrochemical measurements were carried out on an electrochemical analyzer (CHI660E, CH Instruments, Shanghai Chen Hua Co, China) at room temperature by using a three-electrode system consisting of the glassy carbon electrode as the working electrode, a saturated calomel reference electrode, and a platinum auxiliary electrode. Fluorescent emission (FL) spectra were recorded on a Hitachi F-4700 luminescence spectrometer (Hitachi High-Tech Co., Ltd.). Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images, high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) images were obtained using JEOL JEM-2100Plus. Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were recorded on a Magna-560 spectrometer (Nicolet, Madison, WI). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was measured on a Thermo ESCALAB VG Science 250 spectrometer with monochromatic Al Kα excitation. The Raman spectra were analyzed by T64000 of HORIBA Scientific Company.

The preparation of Graphdiyne (GDY) powder has been stated in our previous work [26]. Firstly, the GDY powder (10 mg) was dissolved in ultra-pure water (10 mL) and crushed by ultrasonic (120 W, 30 kHz) for 24 h.The mixture was separated by centrifugation and theliquid was obtained. The obtained is GDQDs. Then, the GDQDs liquid (10 mL) was transferred into polytetrafluoroethylene autoclave and heated at 200 °C for 8 h by adding CH4N2O (2.0 mg).Finally, after 24 h of dialysis, the final N-GDQDs was obtained.

Electrochemical measurements were carried out by using a three-electrode system consisting of the glassy carbon electrode (GCE) as the working electrode, Ag/AgCl electrode as the reference electrode, and a platinum auxiliary electrode. Before the measurement, the bare GCE (3 mm) was cleaned in 0.2 M H2SO4 solution, polished with Al2O3 powder and cleaned by ultrapure water. The cleaning process was to ensure adequate cleaning of the electrode surface. After it was dried, 6 μL of N-GDQDs solution (0.5 mg mL−1) was dropped on the clean surface of GCE and dried at ambient condition. Differentialpulsevoltammetry (DPV) measurements were performed in 0.1 M PBS (pH = 7.0). DA as the redox substances with different concentrations were added to the electrolyte. To obtain well-defined oxidation current peaks, the DPV measurements for DA detection were performed in the potential range from −0.2 to 0.6 V. The amplitude, pulse width, and pulse period were set as 50 mV, 0.05 s and 0.25 s.

In the selective test, 0.1 mol L−1 PBS (pH = 7.0) solution is used as the buffer solution in the chronoamperometric mode, 50 μmol L−1 DA and interfering substances were added to the solution at regular intervals. The concentration of the interfering substances was 1 mmol L−1, the interval was 50 s, and the working voltage was 0.84 V.

In a typical operation, 100 µL of N-GDQDs (0.1 mg mL−1) solution was added to a PBS buffer solution (0.1 mol L−1, pH = 6.0). 100 μL of DA solution with different concentrations were added to the N-GDQDs solution to form a 1 mL solution. The fluorescence intensity of the system in the presence of DA was detected at 319 nm excitation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application test of DA in spiked sample testing
ce:section.ce:para: Standard addition method was used to analyze the practicability of DA detection in human serum. In fluorescent detection, N-GDQDs (0.1 mg mL−1) was added to human serum samples containing different concentrations of DA. After 2 min, the fluorescence spectra were recorded at 319 nm excitation wavelength under room temperature. In electrochemical detection, 6 μL N-GDQDs solution (0.1 mg mL−1) and 2 μL Nafion solution (2 wt%) were added on the surface of dry glassy carbon electrode and dried at room temperature. The serum containing different concentrations of DA was used as electrolyte. DPV measurements of DA detection were carried out in the potential range of −0.2–0.6 V, and the amplitude, pulse width and pulse period were set to 50 mV, 0.05 s and 0.25 s, respectively.

The morphology of the prepared N-GDQDs is characterized by TEM and AFM. It can be seen that N-GDQDs are almost uniformly dispersed and spherical with an average diameter of 5.2 ± 2 nm (Fig. 1 a). Fig. 1b shows the HRTEM image of N-GDQDs, which clearly reveals the good crystallinity of N-GDQDs with a lattice spacing of 0.37 nm, which is basically consistent with the lattice spacing of GDY [27]. It can be seen from the AFM image that the typical topographic height of N-GDQDs is in the range of 4–6 nm (Fig. 1c). The composition of the prepared N-GDQDs is characterized by Raman spectroscopy and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). Fig. 1d shows that the peaks at 1384 and 1589 cm−1 correspond to the D band and G band of N-GDQDs, respectively. The ID/IG values are 0.632, 0.775 and 0.824, respectively. The increased ID/IG value indicates more defects in N-GDQDs are caused by N-doping [28]. The two weak peaks at 1922 and 2186 cm−1 are attributed to the vibration of the conjugated diyne bond (−C≡C−C≡C− ), which is consistent with previous reports [29]. It’s proved that N-GDQDs still have a carbon skeleton similar to that of GDY and GDQDs after the introduction of nitrogen. XPS spectrum shows GDY and GDQDs mainly contain carbon and oxygen elements, and N speak appears after N doping (Fig. S1). In the XPS of N-GDQDs, there are three peaks at 282.1, 398.2 and 529.2 eV, corresponding to C1s, N1s and O1s, respectively. The C1s peak of N-GDQDs (Fig. 1e) can be proposed to be merged into four bonds: C–C (sp2) at 284.4 eV, C–C (sp) at 284.9 eV, C=N/C=O at 286 eV and C–O at 288.5 eV [28]. N 1 s spectrum (Fig. 1f) can be deconvoluted into the pyrrole N (C–N–C, 397.7 eV) and graphite N (N–(C)3, 399.6 eV), which indicate that N atoms have successfully doped into the carbon skeleton of N-GDQDs [30]. The pyrrole N (C–N–C) is formed through substituting the carbon atoms in-between diyne linkages (−C≡C−C≡C−) groups by N atoms [31]. The above analysis and demonstration are consistent with the results of FT-IR (Fig. S2).

Fig. 2 a shows the absorption band of N-GDQDs decreases from 300 nm to 800 nm, and an insignificant absorption band at ca. 320 nm is observed, which is attributed to n-π* transitions of conjugated domains of N-GDQDs [32]. GDQDs and N-GDQDs have similar UV–vis absorption band (Fig. S3). The doping of heteroatoms can change the optical properties of carbon based quantum dots and improve their quantum yield [33]. In our previous work, the quantum yield of the prepared GDQDs is 14.6% [26]. The quantum yield of N-GDQDs is 48.6%, which reveals the introduction of nitrogen greatly improves the optical properties of N-GDQDs. The comparison of fluorescence photos before and after N doping is shown in Fig. S4, which is consistent with the result. Fig. 2b shows the maximum and the best excitation and emission absorption spectra of N-GDQDs. The optimal excitation and emission wavelengths are determined to be 319 nm and 410 nm, respectively. The conclusion is further confirmed by 3D images drawn and scanned at different excitation wavelengths (x axis: 260–340 nm) and emission wavelengths (y axis: 350–550 nm) (Fig. 2c). In addition, the emission spectra at different excitation wavelengths are studied as shown in Fig. 2d (279–359 nm). With the increase of excitation wavelength, the emission peak is gradually red-shifted. It is due to the different band gaps of different sizes of N-GDQDs and the existence of different emission traps or different surface defect states [34]. Fig. 2e and f show the fluorescence changes little after existing in different concentrations of salt solution or continuous UV irradiation for 7000 s, which shows that N-GDQDs have good tolerance to high salt and photobleaching.

The electrocatalytic performance of the modified electrode is studied at a scanning rate of 100 mV s−1. Fig. 3 a shows the peak current of N-GDQDs is higher than that of other modified electrodes because of its large carbon pores and the introduction of nitrogen [35]. And the ΔEp potential difference of N-GDQDs is the smallest, which indicates that it has stronger electrocatalytic oxidation ability than GDY and GDQDs. The electrocatalytic behavior of surface-modified electrodes is investigated through EIS (Fig. 3b). A smaller semicircle with an electron transfer resistance (Rct) value of 424 Ώ for N-GDQDs modified GCE than those of GDQDs (515 Ώ), GDY (678 Ώ) is observed. It shows that N-GDQDs have lower resistance, and it is easier for electrons to transfer to the surface of the modified electrode. Fig. 3c and Fig. S5 displays the CV of bare GCE, GDY, GDQDs, graphene QDs (GQDs) and N-doped graphene QDs (N-GQDs) modified GCE electrode in PBS buffer containing 100 μM DA. The bare GCE, GDY/GCE and GDQDs/GCE, GQDs/GCE and N-GQDs/GCE exhibit weak electrocatalytic ability evident from the observed feeble oxidation peaks at relatively higher over potentials. On the contrary, N-GDQDs/GCE delivers a large peak current originated from the electrocatalytic oxidation for DA. In order to realize the reaction mechanism and dynamic parameters of N-GDQDs/GCE electrode, the effect of scanning rate on the peak current and potential of DA is investigated. The oxidation peaks current intensity (Ci) increases gradually with the increase of scan rate (ν) (Fig. 3d). The Ci is proportional to the square root of the scanning rate (R2 = 0.99), suggesting the oxidation of DA is controlled by diffusion process [36].

The experimental conditions for electrochemical detection of DA including the pH value of the assay and the amount of electrode dripping are optimized, N-GDQDs shows the highest current intensity at pH = 7.0 and 6 μL dripping quantitative amounts (Fig. S6). Under the optimized conditions, the sensitivity of DA electrochemical detection is investigated. Fig. 4 a shows amperometric responses of N-GDQDs modified GCE upon successive addition of DA. With the increase of the concentration of DA, the peak current of N-GDQDs/GCE increases gradually. Clearly, there is a good linear relationship between DA concentration and current in the range of 0.05–240 μM with a correlation coefficient R2 = 0.98 (Fig. 4b). The LOD is 0.02 μM (S/N = 3).

The experimental conditions for fluorescent detection of DA including the pH value of the assay are investigated and optimized, that the best quenching effect occurs when pH = 6.0 (Fig. S7). Fig. 4c shows the fluorescence spectra of N-GDQDs solution with the addition of different DA concentrations. The fluorescence intensity of N-GDQDs at 410 nm decreases with the increase of DA concentration. The curves of quenching ratio F0-F and DA concentration from 0 to 600 μM are performed (Fig. 4d), where F0 and F are the fluorescence intensity of N-GDQDs solution before and after the addition of DA, respectively. The LOD is 0.14 μM (S/N = 3), with a linear range of 0.32–500 μM is obtained (R2 = 0.99). Similar works are summarized in Table. 1 , compared with the other fluorescent and electrochemical sensors, this nanosensor has a wider linear range and a lower LOD. The normal content of DA in healthy people is in the range of 1.3–2.6 μM [10], which is within the linear range of our detection, which shows a clear potential for spiked sample measurement.

In order to verify the selectivity of electrochemical method, chronoamperometric measurement is conducted on the N-GDQDs modified GCE under the potential of 0.84 V, followed by injecting 50 μM DA, and 1 mM potential interfering substances (KCl, NaCl, glycine, L-cystein, sodium nitrate, hydrogen peroxide, glucose, histamine, glutamic acid) the same solution one by one. As presented in Fig. 5 a, excepting a significant current response caused by the addition of DA, the presence of other high-concentration of interfering substances only leads to slight changes in current signal and has negligible influence on current intensity. These results indicate that N-GDQDs/GCE has high selectivity for DA detection. In order to study the reproducibility of the electrode preparation procedure, in the preparation of DA sensor (Fig. 5b), 10 different GCEs with the same modification of 6 μL N-GDQDs are used for the determination of 50 μM DA. The RSD for the between electrode peak currents (average of three determinations on each electrode) is calculated to be 2.8% and the RSD of the currents for 10 replicate measurements on a single electrode in DA is 3.2%. The GCE is polished and dripped with N-GDQDs suspension, and then dried and stored in air for 1 to 5 weeks, and its catalytic activity is measured. As shown in Fig. 5c, after being stored in air for a period of time, the catalytic activity of the DA sensor decreases slightly, and the activity of N-GDQDs still maintains 90% after exposure in air for 2 weeks. The results indicate that the modified electrode has superior stability, high reproducibility and repeatability in both the preparation procedure and the determinations.

The selectivity of the fluorescnce sensor is investigated, and the control experiments are carried out. Firstly, 0.5 mM of DA and 1 mM interfering substances (KCl, NaCl, glycine, L-cystein, sodium nitrate, hydrogen peroxide, glucose, histamine, and glutamic acid) are added to different N-GDQDs solutions, respectively. The changes of fluorescence intensity in different systems were recorded. The results show that the fluorescence intensity of the system has little change after the addition of other interfering substances, and the fluorescence quenching rate of DA to N-GDQDs sensor is much higher than that of other high-concentration of interfering substances (Fig. 5d), indicating that N-GDQDs has a good selectivity for DA detection.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The mechanism of the N-GDQDs sensor to DA in optical and electrochemical mode
ce:section.ce:para: To understand the possible fluorescence sensing quenching mechanism, the lifetime of fluorescence decay of N-GDQDs without and with different concentrations of DA is investigated. The fluorescence decay time of N-GDQDs is 6.53 ns after the addition of DA (Fig. S8), and the decay time doesn’t change obviously when the concentration are 100 µM (6.32 ns) and 200 µM (6.19 ns), which suggests the quenching process belongs to static quenching [49], and a non-fluorescent complex is formed between the fluorescent substance and the quenchant [51]. To test this hypothesis, homologous substances of DA (β-phenethylamine, tyramine, catechol, o-benzoquinone and DA-quinone) are selected to study the complexation state between N-GDQDs and DA (Fig. 6 a). Fig. 6a shows the fluorescence of N-GDQDs isn’t quenched after adding β-phenethylamine, o-benzoquinone, DA-quinone and tyramine. On the contrary, the fluorescence is quenched after adding catechol and DA. Both catechol and DA share the same group, i.e. catechol group. We infer that the catechol group is the source of fluorescence quenching of N-GDQDs [49]. To confirm this, DA-quinone and o-benzoquinone (oxidized by H2O2) are used to investigate the quenching effect by N-GDQDs, and both of them show no quenching effect. Thus, catechol group in DA plays the key role for quenching effect. Phenoxy anions (in PBS solution) in DA show big affinity to pyridinic N sites of N-GDQDs, due to their donor-bridge-acceptor structure, forming a ground-state non-fluorescent complex [49]. The above results indicate catechol group in DA plays a key role in fluorescent detection of DA, that N-GDQDs fluorescent detection method has good specificity.

The mechanism of electrochemical detection of DA by N-GDQDs/GCE (Fig. 6b) is investigated. The oxidation reaction of N-GDQDs toward DA is strongly related to the pyridinic N structure in N-GDQDs and the catechol group in DA. DA can be oxidized to DA-quinone under electric field through N-GDQDs/GCE, and forms a complex between N-GDQDs and DA-quinone [50]. Due to the specific donor-bridge-acceptor structures with a large conjugated domain, the phenoxy anion of DA is easily adsorbed on the surface of N-GDQDs, pyridinic N is the preferred position. Large delocalized π conjugated structures connect N-GDQDs to DA molecules to form complex DA products. Furthermore, this complex excludes the other interferences, resulting in a better selectivity.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Spiked application of N-GDQDs for analysis of DA in human serum samples
ce:section.ce:para: Through the recovery detection of DA in human serum samples, the biological application potential of N-GDQDS solution in spiked samples is explored. Firstly, the detection of DA in human serum by N-GDQDs/GCE under electrochemical detection method is explored, as shown in Table. 2 . The ratio of DA detection to addition in human serum samples ranges from 98.89% to 102.50%, and RSD ranges from 2.22% to 3.33%. The deviation between the electrochemical detection results of N-GDQDs/GCE and the amount of DA is small, indicating that this method has good practical application in the detection of DA in spiked samples, and the detection results are relatively reliable.

In the fluorescent detection of DA in spiked samples based on N-GDQDS solution, the addition detection experiment of human serum samples is also carried out to evaluate the feasibility. Three different concentrations of DA are added to the sample solution. As shown in Table. 2, the recovery range of relevant samples is 97.93–107.03%. The relative standard deviation (n = 3) is between 2.74% and 3.70%. The high recovery rate and standard deviation indicate the reliability of N-GDQDs solution for the fluorescent detection of DA in practical samples, which proves that the method has great application potential in the clinical detection of DA.

N-GDQDs are successfully prepared from urea and GDY powder by ultrasonic crushing and hydrothermal method. The introduction of nitrogen greatly improves the fluorescence performance with the quantum yield enhanced from 14.6% to 48.6%, and facilitates electron transferring of N-GDQDs due to improved conductivity. A novel electrochemical and fluorescent detection mechanism is designed for DA detection. In the electrochemical detection, the linear range is 0.05–240 μM and the LOD is 0.02 μM. The LOD of DA by fluorescent method is 0.14 μM, and the linear range is 0.32–500 μM. Either mode shows a similar level for DA detection, but the sensor significantly improves the accuracy and reliability of detection. In addition, the selective exploration of detection and the recovery experiment in spiked serum samples show that this method has a clear potential for the spiked-sampled detection.

Qiang Bai: Conceptualization, Methodology, Formal analysis, Investigation, Data curation, Writing – original draft. Hongyang Luo: Conceptualization, Methodology, Formal analysis, Investigation, Data curation, Writing – original draft. Xuetao Yi: Methodology, Investigation. Shugao Shi: Methodology, Investigation. Lina Wang: Resources, Funding acquisition. Manhong Liu: Data curation, Resources. Fanglin Du: Writing – review & editing. Zhugen Yang: Formal analysis, Data curation, Supervision, Resources, Writing – review & editing, Project administration. Ning Sui: Resources, Supervision, Funding acquisition, Project administration.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No. 21501106, 31800800), Natural Science Foundation of Shandong Province (Grant No. ZR2020MB026, ZR202103010467), and Qingdao Municipal Science and Technology Commission (Grant No. 19-6-2-73-cg, 16-5-1-86-jch).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.microc.2022.107521.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
